summary: This guest post by Becky Jaffe presents a curated reading list of Black history and literature as a response to white supremacy, particularly in the context of the 2017 political climate. The author arranges book and documentary titles into a poem format, creating an artistic framework for education. The list spans African and African American literature, slave narratives, civil rights history, and contemporary works. Jaffe explicitly frames this educational approach as resistance, urging readers not to 'give an ideological inch to the white nationalists in the white house.' The post serves as both a practical resource and a call to action through learning.
key_points: Education and learning Black history is presented as a form of resistance against white supremacy
The reading list encompasses diverse formats including novels, memoirs, slave narratives, poetry, and documentaries
Works span from historical slave narratives (Frederick Douglass, Harriet Jacobs) to contemporary voices (Ta-Nehisi Coates, Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie)
The creative presentation as a poem demonstrates how book titles themselves carry powerful meaning
The list includes both African and African American perspectives, broadening the scope beyond U.S. history
money_quotes: One way to disarm the dangerous ideology of white supremacy is to teach and learn Black history inside and outside of the classroom.
Let us not give an ideological inch to the white nationalists in the white house.
We Are the Ones We Have Been Waiting For
Anything We Love Can Be Saved
key_insight: Countering dangerous ideologies requires proactive education, and the titles of freedom literature themselves form a powerful collective statement when read together.
